question: What does inheritance allow in Java?
options: A. A class to contain only static methods
B. A class to reuse and extend another class
C. A class to inherit from many classes directly
D. A class to avoid using constructors
explanation: Inheritance lets a child class receive fields and methods from a parent class and add or change behavior.

question: Which keyword is used for inheritance in Java?
explanation: A class inherits from another using extends, for example: class Dog extends Animal.

question: Which statement is true about Java inheritance?
options: A. A class can extend many classes
B. A class can extend only one class
C. A class cannot inherit methods
D. A class must always extend Object explicitly
explanation: Java supports single inheritance between classes — a class can directly extend only one other class.

question: When is inheritance logically appropriate?
options: A. When class B 'has a' class A
B. When class B 'uses' class A
C. When class B 'is a type of' class A
D. When class B contains an array of A
explanation: Inheritance represents an 'is-a' relationship, such as Dog is an Animal.

question: What happens when an object of a child class is created?
options: A. Only the child constructor runs
B. Only the parent constructor runs
C. The parent part is constructed before the child part
D. The child part is constructed before the parent part
explanation: Java must first construct the parent part of the object before continuing with the child constructor.

question: What is automatically inserted if a constructor does not explicitly call super(...) or this(...)?
explanation: Java automatically inserts super() as the first line if no explicit constructor call appears.

question: What must be true about a call to super(...) inside a constructor?
options: A. It must be the last line
C. It must be the first line
D. It can only be used in abstract classes
explanation: A super(...) call must be the very first statement in a child constructor.

question: What does super refer to inside a child class?
options: A. The current object viewed as the child class only
B. The parent-class part of the current object
C. A new separate object of the parent class
explanation: super is used to access the parent-class constructor or parent-class methods from inside the child class.

question: What does this refer to inside a method or constructor?
explanation: this refers to the current object on which the method or constructor is being executed.

question: In a hierarchy Bulldog extends Dog extends Animal, what does super inside Bulldog refer to directly?
explanation: super always refers to the direct parent class. Inside Bulldog, that is Dog.

question: Can constructors be inherited in Java?
options: B. Yes, only if they are public
D. Yes, only if they are protected
explanation: Constructors are never inherited. Each class must define its own constructors.

question: What happens if a parent class has no no-argument constructor and the child constructor does not call super(...) explicitly?
options: B. Java creates an empty parent constructor automatically
explanation: Java tries to insert super() automatically. If no no-arg constructor exists in the parent, compilation fails.

question: Which fields does a child class inherit from its parent?
options: B. All fields, but private fields are not directly accessible in the child
explanation: The child object contains all parent fields, but private ones cannot be accessed directly from child code.

options: B. w is private in A and not directly accessible in B
explanation: A private field from the parent is not directly accessible inside the child class.

question: What does the protected access modifier allow?
options: A. Access only from the same method
C. Access from the class itself, its subclasses, and classes in the same package
explanation: protected is more open than private — it allows access from subclasses and same-package classes.

question: What is method overriding?
options: A. Defining a method with the same name but different parameters
B. Redefining an inherited method in a child class with the same signature
explanation: Overriding happens when a child class provides a new implementation for an inherited method with the same name and parameters.

question: Which type of method can be overridden?
explanation: Only non-private instance methods can be overridden. Private and static methods are not polymorphically overridden.

question: Is the @Override annotation required for method overriding?
options: B. No, but it helps the compiler detect mistakes
explanation: @Override is optional but recommended — the compiler will warn you if the method doesn't actually override anything.
Dog overrides makeSound(), so the Dog version runs and prints Bark.
The parent constructor always runs first. Animal prints first, then Dog.
super.makeSound() calls the parent version first, then the child adds its own output.

question: What does the final keyword mean when applied to a class?
options: A. The class cannot contain constructors
B. The class cannot be extended by another class
C. The class cannot have fields
explanation: A final class cannot be subclassed. String is a well-known example of a final class in Java.

question: What does the final keyword mean when applied to a method?
options: A. The method cannot be called
B. The method cannot return a value
C. The method cannot be overridden in a subclass
explanation: A final method can be inherited and called, but no subclass can override it.

question: What is polymorphism in Java?
options: A. A reference can only point to one exact class
B. A parent reference can point to a child object
C. A child reference can always point to a parent object
D. Java chooses methods only by reference type
explanation: Polymorphism allows a parent-type reference to hold a child-type object, and the correct overridden method runs at runtime.

question: Which assignment is valid given: class Animal {} and class Dog extends Animal {}?
explanation: A Dog is an Animal, so an Animal reference can legally point to a Dog object. The reverse is not automatically valid.

question: Why is Dog d = new Animal() invalid?
options: A. Because every Animal is always a Dog
B. Because not every Animal is a Dog — Animal may lack Dog-specific behavior
C. Because Dog cannot extend Animal
D. Because constructors cannot be called this way
explanation: A child reference requires an actual child object. An Animal may not have the specific fields and methods that Dog defines.
For overridden instance methods, Java uses dynamic dispatch — the actual object type is C, so C.func() returns 300.
C does not override func(), so Java uses the closest inherited version, which is B.func() returning 20.
The reference is B but the actual object is C. Dynamic dispatch calls C.func() which returns 300.

question: What determines which overridden instance method runs at runtime?
options: B. The actual object type at runtime
explanation: Polymorphism uses dynamic dispatch: the JVM looks at the real object type to decide which overridden method to call.

question: What determines which field is accessed when using a reference?
options: A. The actual object type at runtime
B. The declared reference type at compile time
explanation: Fields are not polymorphic. The compiler resolves field access based on the declared type of the reference.
Fields are not polymorphic. Since the reference is declared as Parent, Parent.num (which is 1) is accessed.

question: What is true about static methods and polymorphism?
options: A. Static methods are dynamically dispatched like instance methods
B. Static methods are resolved according to the reference or class type
C. Static methods cannot be inherited at all
D. Static methods must always be private
explanation: Static methods do not use dynamic dispatch. They are resolved at compile time based on the reference or class type.
Static methods are resolved by the reference type. p is declared as Parent, so Parent.getNum() returns 300.

question: What is Object in Java?
options: A. A regular class with no special importance
B. The superclass of all classes in Java
C. A keyword for creating arrays
D. A class that only works with strings
explanation: Every Java class inherits from Object directly or indirectly. It is the root of the entire class hierarchy.

question: If a class does not explicitly extend another class, what does it extend?
explanation: Java automatically makes every class extend Object if no other parent is specified.

question: Which method is defined in the Object class?
explanation: toString() is defined in Object, so every Java object inherits it and can override it.

options: A. Because Object defines toString() which println can call
C. Because Person is not really an object
D. Because println ignores object types
explanation: println calls toString() on the object. Since toString() is defined in Object, this always works.

options: A. The real object is not a Person
B. getName() is not defined in the declared type Object
C. Object cannot store a Person
explanation: The compiler uses the reference type to check available methods. Object does not declare getName(), so the call is rejected.

question: How can the previous code be fixed to call getName()?
explanation: Downcasting with (Person) tells the compiler to treat o as a Person, giving access to getName().

question: What does the instanceof operator check?
options: A. Whether a variable is static
B. Whether an object is an instance of a given class or subclass
C. Whether a class has a constructor
D. Whether two objects have the same field values
explanation: instanceof returns true if the object on the left belongs to the class or any subclass on the right.
The actual object is a Dog, so instanceof Dog returns true even though the reference is Animal.
Every Dog is also an Animal, so instanceof Animal returns true.

question: Why is instanceof useful before downcasting?
options: A. It automatically changes the object type
B. It lets you verify the object is really of that child type before casting
C. It creates a new object of the target type
explanation: Checking instanceof before a downcast prevents a ClassCastException at runtime if the object is not the expected type.

question: What is upcasting?
options: A. Casting from a parent reference to a child reference
B. Treating a child object as an object of its parent type
C. Casting from int to double
explanation: Upcasting assigns a child object to a parent-type reference, such as Animal a = new Dog(). It is always safe and implicit.

question: What is downcasting?
options: A. Treating a parent-type reference as a child-type reference
B. Treating a child as an Object
explanation: Downcasting converts a parent-type reference back to a more specific child type, and requires an explicit cast.

question: Which line is an example of downcasting?
explanation: Dog d = (Dog) a casts a parent-type reference back down to the child type Dog. This requires an explicit cast.

options: B. Compilation error because casting is never allowed
C. Compiles but throws a ClassCastException at runtime
D. Converts the Cat object into a Dog object
explanation: The cast compiles since Dog and Animal are related, but at runtime the object is a Cat, causing a ClassCastException.

question: In a Person[] array holding Person, Student, and Clown objects, why can draw() be called on every element if draw() is defined in Person?
options: B. Because every element is guaranteed to have draw() through the Person type
explanation: The array type is Person[], so the compiler allows any method in Person. At runtime, overridden versions in subclasses run automatically.

options: A. The actual object is not a Clown
B. The reference type Person does not define tellJoke()
explanation: The compiler checks the reference type. Person does not declare tellJoke(), so a downcast is required: ((Clown)p).tellJoke();
